From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

1903 four-act drama by George Bernard Shaw

| Man and Superman | |
| --- | --- |
| [Lillah McCarthy](/wiki/Lillah_McCarthy "Lillah McCarthy") as Ann Whitefield and [Harley Granville Barker](/wiki/Harley_Granville_Barker "Harley Granville Barker") as John Tanner, 1905 | |
| Written by | [Bernard Shaw](/wiki/George_Bernard_Shaw "George Bernard Shaw") |
| Date premiered | 21 May 1905 |
| Place premiered | [Court Theatre](/wiki/Royal_Court_Theatre "Royal Court Theatre"), London |
| Original language | English |
| Genre | "Comedy and philosophy" |

***Man and Superman*** is a four-act [drama](/wiki/Drama "Drama") written by [Bernard Shaw](/wiki/George_Bernard_Shaw "George Bernard Shaw") in 1903 and premiered at the [Court Theatre](/wiki/Royal_Court_Theatre "Royal Court Theatre") in London on 21 May 1905. The first production – like most subsequent ones – omitted a dream sequence, *Don Juan in Hell*, from the third act. That episode was first given at the Court in 1907. The play humorously illustrates the theories of the philosopher [Friedrich Nietzsche](/wiki/Friedrich_Nietzsche "Friedrich Nietzsche") that humanity will evolve to superman status and that it is women, rather than men, who drive the process.

The play was staged with success on [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_theatre "Broadway theatre") within months of the [West End](/wiki/West_End_theatre "West End theatre") premiere and has been revived many times in Britain, North America and elsewhere. It has also been adapted for radio and television.

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Man_and_Superman&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background")]

Shaw established a reputation as a dramatist during the 1890s with plays including *[Arms and the Man](/wiki/Arms_and_the_Man "Arms and the Man")* (1894), *[The Devil's Disciple](/wiki/The_Devil%27s_Disciple_(play) "The Devil's Disciple (play)")* (1897) and *[Caesar and Cleopatra](/wiki/Caesar_and_Cleopatra_(play) "Caesar and Cleopatra (play)")* (1898) and by the start of the twentieth century he was recognised as an important figure in the British theatre.[[1]](#cite_note-1) The drama critic [A. B. Walkley](/wiki/Arthur_Bingham_Walkley "Arthur Bingham Walkley") suggested that Shaw should write a play based on the [Don Juan](/wiki/Don_Juan "Don Juan") legend.[[n 1]](#cite_note-3) In May 1900 Shaw began sketching a Parliament in Hell between Don Juan and the Devil. His biographer [Michael Holroyd](/wiki/Michael_Holroyd "Michael Holroyd") writes, "Around the [Socratic](/wiki/Socratic_method "Socratic method") debate he composed a three-act comedy, completing the scenario between 2 July and 8 October 1901. … in June 1902, this many-layered work, now called *Man and Superman. A Comedy and a Philosophy*, was finished". Shaw immediately worked on a revised draft and in January 1903 he read it aloud to his friends [Beatrice](/wiki/Beatrice_Webb "Beatrice Webb") and [Sidney Webb](/wiki/Sidney_Webb,_1st_Baron_Passfield "Sidney Webb, 1st Baron Passfield"), who were enthusiastic about the piece.[[3]](#cite_note-4)

## First productions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Man_and_Superman&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: First productions")]

The main body of the play – without the Act 3 dream sequence *Don Juan in Hell* – was first given by the [Incorporated Stage Society](/wiki/Incorporated_Stage_Society "Incorporated Stage Society") at a matinee at the [Court Theatre](/wiki/Royal_Court_Theatre "Royal Court Theatre"), London, on 21 May 1905. From the third performance (23 May) the play was presented by the [Granville Barker](/wiki/Harley_Granville_Barker "Harley Granville Barker")-[Vedrenne](/wiki/John_Eugene_Vedrenne "John Eugene Vedrenne") management as part of a series of matinees they were presenting. There were fourteen matinee performances of the play in this first run.[[4]](#cite_note-5) The piece was given at 68 evening and matinee performances at the Court between 23 October and 30 December 1905 with mainly the original cast.[[5]](#cite_note-w413-6) Further performances were presented at the same theatre by the same management in 1906 (61 performances) and 1907 (35 performances).[[6]](#cite_note-7)

The first performance of the *Don Juan in Hell* episode was at the Court on 4 June 1907 in a series of eight matinee performances presented by the Granville Barker-Vedrenne management, with Loraine as Don Juan, Michael Sherbrook as the statue, [Norman McKinnel](/wiki/Norman_McKinnel "Norman McKinnel") as the Devil and McCarthy as Doña Ana.[[10]](#cite_note-11)

## Summary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Man_and_Superman&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Summary")]

Mr Whitefield has recently died, and his [will](/wiki/Will_and_testament "Will and testament") indicates that his daughter Ann should be left in the care of two men, Roebuck Ramsden and John Tanner. Ramsden, a venerable old man, distrusts John Tanner, an eloquent youth with revolutionary ideas, whom Shaw's stage directions describe as "prodigiously fluent of speech, restless, excitable (mark the snorting nostril and the restless [blue eye](/wiki/Blue_eye "Blue eye"), just the thirty-secondth of an inch too wide open), possibly a little mad".[[11]](#cite_note-John-12) In spite of what Ramsden says, Ann accepts Tanner as her [guardian](/wiki/Legal_guardian "Legal guardian"), though Tanner does not want the position at all. She also challenges Tanner's revolutionary beliefs with her own ideas. Despite Tanner's professed dedication to [anarchy](/wiki/Anarchy "Anarchy"), he is unable to disarm Ann's charm, and she ultimately persuades him to marry her,[[12]](#cite_note-ann-13) choosing him over her more persistent suitor, a young man, Tanner's friend, named Octavius Robinson.

## Characters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Man_and_Superman&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Characters")]

* **Roebuck Ramsden**, an ageing civil reformer who was friend to the late Mr Whitefield. He corresponds to the statue in the [Don Juan](/wiki/Don_Juan "Don Juan") myth, who is in turn the representation of the spirit of Don Gonzalo, the father of Doña Ana (in Act III, Shaw writes of The Statue, "His voice, save for a much more distinguished intonation, is so like the voice of Roebuck Ramsden").
* **Octavius Robinson**, an amiable young man who is in love with Ann Whitefield. Brother to Violet Robinson. He represents "Don Ottavio" from the Don Juan myth.

[![youngish, clean-shaven white man in a light-coloured suit, seated on a garden step, looking pensive](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/8/8f/Robert-loraine-Jack-tanner-1911.png/250px-Robert-loraine-Jack-tanner-1911.png)](/wiki/File:Robert-loraine-Jack-tanner-1911.png)

[Robert Loraine](/wiki/Robert_Loraine "Robert Loraine") as Jack Tanner, 1911

* **John "Jack" Tanner**, a well-educated, well-spoken man who takes everything seriously, including himself; a "political firebrand and confirmed bachelor."[[13]](#cite_note-blackbird-14) Allegedly the descendant of [Don Juan](/wiki/Don_Juan "Don Juan"), as well as the modern representation of the Don Juan character (in Act III, Shaw notes Don Juan's resemblance to Tanner: "Besides, in the brief lifting of his face, now hidden by his hat brim, there was a curious suggestion of Tanner. A more critical, fastidious, handsome face, paler and colder, without Tanner's impetuous credulity and enthusiasm, and without a touch of his modern plutocratic vulgarity, but still a resemblance, even an identity"). The name "John Tanner" is an [anglicisation](/wiki/Anglicisation "Anglicisation") of the Spanish name "Juan Tenorio", which is the full name of Don Juan.[[14]](#cite_note-15)
* **Henry Straker**, chauffeur with a [cockney](/wiki/Cockney "Cockney") accent (representing Leporello from [the Mozart opera](/wiki/Don_Giovanni "Don Giovanni")).[[15]](#cite_note-dedication-16)
* **Hector Malone Jr**, an American gentleman who is secretly married to Violet Robinson.
* **Hector Malone Sr**, an elderly gentleman who has worked hard throughout his life to attain a high social status in which he now takes pride.
* **Ann Whitefield**, a young woman, graceful, somewhat enigmatic. She corresponds to the character Doña Ana in the Don Juan myth (in Act III, Shaw's stage direction refers to Doña Ana de Ulloa as "so handsome that in the radiance into which her dull yellow halo has suddenly lightened one might almost mistake her for Ann Whitefield").
* **Mrs Whitefield**, mother of Ann, and widow of the late Mr Whitefield.
* **Susan Ramsden**, unmarried sister of Roebuck Ramsden.
* **Violet Robinson**, sister of Octavius Robinson. She has been secretly married to Hector Malone Jr.
* **Mendoza**, an [anarchist](/wiki/Anarchist "Anarchist") who collaborates with Tanner. Mendoza is the "President of the League of the Sierra," a self-described [brigand](/wiki/Brigand "Brigand"). He corresponds to Shaw's conception of the [Devil](/wiki/Devil "Devil") as he would be portrayed in the Don Juan myth (Shaw writes of "The Devil" in Act III: "A scarlet halo begins to glow; and into it the Devil rises, very [Mephistophelean](/wiki/Mephistophelean "Mephistophelean"), and not at all unlike Mendoza, though not so interesting").

## Interpretation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Man_and_Superman&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Interpretation")]

### Act III / Don Juan in Hell

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Man_and_Superman&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Act III / Don Juan in Hell")]

The long third act of the play, which shows Don Juan himself having a conversation with several characters in [Hell](/wiki/Hell "Hell"), is often cut. Charles A. Berst observes of Act III:

Paradoxically, the act is both extraneous and central to the drama which surrounds it. It can be dispensed with, and usually is, on grounds that it is just too long to include in an already full-length play. More significantly, it is in some aspects a digression, operates in a different mode from the rest of the material, delays the immediate well-made story line, and much of its subject matter is already implicit in the rest of the play. The play performs well without it.[[16]](#cite_note-extra-17)

*Don Juan in Hell* consists of a philosophical debate between Don Juan (played by the same actor who plays Jack Tanner), and the [Devil](/wiki/Devil "Devil"), with Doña Ana (Ann) and the [Statue](/wiki/Statue "Statue") of Don Gonzalo, Ana's father (Roebuck Ramsden) looking on. This third act is sometimes performed separately as a play in its own right. In 1974–1975, [Kurt Kasznar](/wiki/Kurt_Kasznar "Kurt Kasznar"), [Myrna Loy](/wiki/Myrna_Loy "Myrna Loy"), [Edward Mulhare](/wiki/Edward_Mulhare "Edward Mulhare") and [Ricardo Montalbán](/wiki/Ricardo_Montalb%C3%A1n "Ricardo Montalbán") toured nationwide in [John Houseman](/wiki/John_Houseman "John Houseman")'s production, playing 158 cities in six months.[[17]](#cite_note-18)

### Ideas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Man_and_Superman&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Ideas")]

The play is described by Shaw as "a comedy and a philosophy".[[18]](#cite_note-odp-19) Although it can be performed as a light [comedy of manners](/wiki/Comedy_of_manners "Comedy of manners"), Shaw intended the drama to be something deeper, as suggested by the title, which comes from [Friedrich Nietzsche](/wiki/Friedrich_Nietzsche "Friedrich Nietzsche")'s philosophical ideas about the "[Übermensch](/wiki/%C3%9Cbermensch "Übermensch")" (although Shaw distances himself from Nietzsche by placing the philosopher at the very end of a long list of influences).[[15]](#cite_note-dedication-16)[[19]](#cite_note-20)[[20]](#cite_note-21) As Shaw notes in his "Epistle Dedicatory" (dedication to the critic A. B. Walkley) he wrote the play as "a pretext for a propaganda of our own views of life".[[15]](#cite_note-dedication-16) The plot centres on John Tanner, author of "The Revolutionist's Handbook and Pocket Companion", which is published with the play as a 58-page appendix. Both in the play and in the "Handbook" Shaw takes Nietzsche's theme that mankind is evolving, through [natural selection](/wiki/Natural_selection "Natural selection"), towards "superman" and develops the argument to suggest that the prime mover in selection is the woman: Ann Whitefield makes persistent efforts to entice Tanner to marry her yet he remains a bachelor. As Shaw himself puts it: "Don Juan had changed his sex and become Dona Juana, breaking out of the Doll's House and asserting herself as an individual".[[15]](#cite_note-dedication-16)[[21]](#cite_note-22) This is an explicit, intended reversal of [Tirso de Molina](/wiki/Tirso_de_Molina "Tirso de Molina")'s play *[The Trickster of Seville and the Stone Guest](/wiki/The_Trickster_of_Seville_and_the_Stone_Guest "The Trickster of Seville and the Stone Guest")*, more widely known as the source of [Da Ponte's](/wiki/Lorenzo_Da_Ponte "Lorenzo Da Ponte") *[Don Giovanni](/wiki/Don_Giovanni "Don Giovanni")*; here Ann, representing Doña Ana, is the predator – "Don Juan is the quarry instead of the huntsman," as Shaw notes.[[15]](#cite_note-dedication-16)[[22]](#cite_note-23)

Ann is referred to as "the [Life Force](/wiki/Energy_(esotericism) "Energy (esotericism)")" and represents Shaw's view that in every culture, it is the women who force the men to marry them rather than the men who take the initiative.[[15]](#cite_note-dedication-16) Sally Peters Vogt proposes: "Thematically, the fluid Don Juan myth becomes a favorable milieu for [Creative Evolution](/wiki/George_Bernard_Shaw#CITEREFReligion:_Creative_Revolutionary:_Time,_December_1950 "George Bernard Shaw")", and that "the legend ... becomes in *Man and Superman* the vehicle through which Shaw communicates his cosmic philosophy".[[23]](#cite_note-creative-24)

## Critical reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Man_and_Superman&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Critical reception")]

After the Broadway premiere in 1905 the New York correspondent of *[The Daily Telegraph](/wiki/The_Daily_Telegraph "The Daily Telegraph")* reported:

It is hardly any exaggeration to say that Mr [Charles Frohman](/wiki/Charles_Frohman "Charles Frohman")'s production of Mr G. Bernard Shaw's *Man and Superman* at the Hudson Theatre last night has taken theatrical critics here by storm. Brilliant, witty, audacious, full of life and sharp epigram, strictly unconventional, iridescent Bernardian farce at its most intellectual stage – these are some of the adjectives and descriptions culled at random from the headlines of this morning's leading papers, describing the first night's performance.[[24]](#cite_note-dt-25)

*[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* said, "It was a brilliant comedy, acted to perfection".[[24]](#cite_note-dt-25)

*The Oxford Dictionary of Plays* comments that *Man and Superman* can be enjoyed "as a comedy in which the girl gets her man", or, "especially through the *Don Juan in Hell* scenes ... as a philosophical debate which informs the comic outcome". The dictionary adds

The comedy sparkles with wit and presents well-drawn characters, even in the sub-plot involving Ann's sister. The philosophical debates, though protracted for modern theatrical taste, are lively and thought-provoking in their discussion of the Life Force, derived from the thinking of Nietzsche and [Bergson](/wiki/Bergson "Bergson").[[18]](#cite_note-odp-19)

In the view of the literary critic [John Sutherland](/wiki/John_Sutherland_(author) "John Sutherland (author)"), "the story is full of coincidences and melodramatic happenings, but there is invariably something ironic or paradoxical about them". For Sutherland the play is "heavily imbued with Shavian metaphysics" and he notes that Shaw describes the *Don Juan in Hell* episode as "a new [book of Genesis](/wiki/Book_of_Genesis "Book of Genesis") for the Bible of the Evolutionists.[[25]](#cite_note-26)

## Revivals and adaptations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Man_and_Superman&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Revivals and adaptations")]

In most productions the *Don Juan in Hell* sequence is omitted.[[18]](#cite_note-odp-19)[[26]](#cite_note-bordman-27)

### Britain

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Man_and_Superman&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Britain")]

#### 1911 to 1938

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Man_and_Superman&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: 1911 to 1938")]

The first London revival after the runs at the Court was at the [Criterion Theatre](/wiki/Criterion_Theatre "Criterion Theatre") in 1911. Loraine again played Jack, and [Pauline Chase](/wiki/Pauline_Chase "Pauline Chase") was Ann.[[27]](#cite_note-28) It ran for 167 performances.[[28]](#cite_note-29) The first London production of the full play, including the *Don Juan in Hell* sequence, was given at the Regent Theatre in October 1925.[[29]](#cite_note-ww-30)

There were further revivals of the play at the [Kingsway Theatre](/wiki/Novelty_Theatre "Novelty Theatre") in 1927, the Court in 1930, the Kingsway in 1931 and the [Cambridge Theatre](/wiki/Cambridge_Theatre "Cambridge Theatre") in 1935.[[29]](#cite_note-ww-30) In all these revivals [Esmé Percy](/wiki/Esm%C3%A9_Percy "Esmé Percy") played Jack opposite leading ladies including [Gwen Ffrangcon-Davies](/wiki/Gwen_Ffrangcon-Davies "Gwen Ffrangcon-Davies") and [Rosalinde Fuller](/wiki/Rosalinde_Fuller "Rosalinde Fuller").[[30]](#cite_note-31) The last pre-war London production was at the [Old Vic](/wiki/Old_Vic "Old Vic") in 1938.[[29]](#cite_note-ww-30) Lewis Casson directed, [Anthony Quayle](/wiki/Anthony_Quayle "Anthony Quayle") played Jack and Valerie Tudor played Ann.[[31]](#cite_note-32)

#### 1951 to present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Man_and_Superman&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: 1951 to present")]

The first West End revival after the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War") was at the [New Theatre](/wiki/No%C3%ABl_Coward_Theatre "Noël Coward Theatre") in 1951.[[29]](#cite_note-ww-30) It starred [John Clements](/wiki/John_Clements_(actor) "John Clements (actor)") and [Kay Hammond](/wiki/Kay_Hammond "Kay Hammond") as the protagonists.[[32]](#cite_note-33) A revival at the [Prince's](/wiki/Shaftesbury_Theatre "Shaftesbury Theatre") in the same year included *Don Juan in Hell*.[[29]](#cite_note-ww-30)

A production at the [Arts Theatre](/wiki/Arts_Theatre "Arts Theatre") in 1965 starred [Alan Badel](/wiki/Alan_Badel "Alan Badel") and [Siân Phillips](/wiki/Si%C3%A2n_Phillips "Siân Phillips").[[33]](#cite_note-34) The [Royal Shakespeare Company](/wiki/Royal_Shakespeare_Company "Royal Shakespeare Company") staged the play in 1977, first at the Malvern Festival and then on tour, and finally at the [Savoy Theatre](/wiki/Savoy_Theatre "Savoy Theatre") in London. [Richard Pasco](/wiki/Richard_Pasco "Richard Pasco") played Jack and [Susan Hampshire](/wiki/Susan_Hampshire "Susan Hampshire") Ann.[[34]](#cite_note-35) The [National Theatre](/wiki/Royal_National_Theatre "Royal National Theatre") has twice staged the piece in full, including the *Don Juan in Hell* episode. In the first production (1981) [Daniel Massey](/wiki/Daniel_Massey_(actor) "Daniel Massey (actor)") was Jack/Juan and [Penelope Wilton](/wiki/Penelope_Wilton "Penelope Wilton") Ann/Ana; in the second (2015) [Ralph Fiennes](/wiki/Ralph_Fiennes "Ralph Fiennes") was Jack/Juan and [Indira Varma](/wiki/Indira_Varma "Indira Varma") Ann/Ana.[[35]](#cite_note-36) Between those productions [Peter O'Toole](/wiki/Peter_O%27Toole "Peter O'Toole") starred in a 1982 production that toured and then played at the [Haymarket Theatre](/wiki/Haymarket_Theatre "Haymarket Theatre") in London.[[36]](#cite_note-37)

### Ireland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Man_and_Superman&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Ireland")]

The [Abbey Theatre](/wiki/Abbey_Theatre "Abbey Theatre") presented three productions of the play, in 1917, 1925 and 1927. They were directed by J. Augustus Keogh, Michael J. Dolan and [Lennox Robinson](/wiki/Lennox_Robinson "Lennox Robinson").[[37]](#cite_note-38) In 2012 the [Irish Repertory Theatre](/wiki/Irish_Repertory_Theatre "Irish Repertory Theatre") and Gingold Theatrical Group presented a revival directed and adapted by [David Staller](/wiki/David_Staller "David Staller").[[38]](#cite_note-39)

### France

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Man_and_Superman&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: France")]

*[Les Archives du spectacle](/wiki/Les_Archives_du_spectacle "Les Archives du spectacle")* list two productions of *Don Juan in Hell* (*Don Juan aux enfers*), in a translation by [André Maurois](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Maurois "André Maurois"), but no productions of the main *Man and Superman* in France are recorded.[[39]](#cite_note-40)

### North America

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Man_and_Superman&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: North America")]

The [Internet Broadway Database](/wiki/Internet_Broadway_Database "Internet Broadway Database") lists five productions of the play. The first, in 1905, starred Loraine and [Fay Davis](/wiki/Fay_Davis "Fay Davis") and ran for 192 performances.[[40]](#cite_note-ibdb-41) A revival directed by and starring [Maurice Evans](/wiki/Maurice_Evans_(actor) "Maurice Evans (actor)") ran for 295 performances in 1947–48.[[40]](#cite_note-ibdb-41) *Don Juan in Hell* was given an all‐star reading with considerable success in 1951 with [Charles Boyer](/wiki/Charles_Boyer "Charles Boyer"), [Charles Laughton](/wiki/Charles_Laughton "Charles Laughton"), [Cedric Hardwicke](/wiki/Cedric_Hardwicke "Cedric Hardwicke"), and [Agnes Moorehead](/wiki/Agnes_Moorehead "Agnes Moorehead"). The complete play was mounted by the [Phoenix Theatre](/wiki/Phoenix_Theatre_(New_York_City) "Phoenix Theatre (New York City)") in 1964 with [Ellis Rabb](/wiki/Ellis_Rabb "Ellis Rabb"), [Rosemary Harris](/wiki/Rosemary_Harris "Rosemary Harris") and [Nancy Marchand](/wiki/Nancy_Marchand "Nancy Marchand"), and by the [Circle in the Square](/wiki/Circle_in_the_Square "Circle in the Square") in 1978 with [George Grizzard](/wiki/George_Grizzard "George Grizzard"), Ann Sachs, and [Philip Bosco](/wiki/Philip_Bosco "Philip Bosco").[[26]](#cite_note-bordman-27)

In 1982 [David Wheeler](/wiki/David_Wheeler_(stage_director) "David Wheeler (stage director)") directed a production at the [Charles Playhouse](/wiki/Charles_Playhouse "Charles Playhouse") in Boston, starring [Richard Jordan](/wiki/Richard_Jordan "Richard Jordan") and [Diane Salinger](/wiki/Diane_Salinger "Diane Salinger"). During rehearsals, the play was gradually whittled down to a three-hour length, but the "Don Juan in Hell" sequence survived intact.[[41]](#cite_note-42) In 1990 [South Coast Repertory](/wiki/South_Coast_Repertory "South Coast Repertory") in [Costa Mesa, California](/wiki/Costa_Mesa,_California "Costa Mesa, California") staged a production, with the *Don Juan in Hell* act included, directed by Martin Benson and starring [John de Lancie](/wiki/John_de_Lancie "John de Lancie") as Jack Tanner and his wife Marnie Mosiman as Ann Whitefield.[[42]](#cite_note-43)

The [Shaw Festival](/wiki/Shaw_Festival "Shaw Festival") in Canada opened its first season (1962) with *Don Juan in Hell* and presented the main play in 1966 with [Barry Morse](/wiki/Barry_Morse "Barry Morse") and [Pat Galloway](/wiki/Pat_Galloway "Pat Galloway") as the protagonists (1966). In two later festival productions, which included *Don Juan in Hell*, the leading roles were played by [Ian Richardson](/wiki/Ian_Richardson "Ian Richardson") and [Carole Shelley](/wiki/Carole_Shelley "Carole Shelley") (1977), and [Michael Ball](/wiki/Michael_Ball_(actor) "Michael Ball (actor)") and [Kate Trotter](/wiki/Kate_Trotter "Kate Trotter") (1989).[[43]](#cite_note-44) The festival again staged the work in 2004 with [Ben Carlson](/wiki/Ben_Carlson "Ben Carlson") and Fiona Blair as Tanner and Ann,[[44]](#cite_note-45) and in 2019 with Gray Powell and [Sara Topham](/wiki/Sara_Topham "Sara Topham").[[45]](#cite_note-46)

### Radio

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Man_and_Superman&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Radio")]

In 1946, the [BBC Third Programme](/wiki/BBC_Third_Programme "BBC Third Programme") broadcast the entire play over the wireless for the first time. The production was directed by Peter Watts. It starred John Garside, [Leonard Sachs](/wiki/Leonard_Sachs "Leonard Sachs"), [Sebastian Shaw](/wiki/Sebastian_Shaw_(actor) "Sebastian Shaw (actor)") and Grizelda Hervey among others.[[46]](#cite_note-47) A 1965 adaptation starred [Robert Hardy](/wiki/Robert_Hardy "Robert Hardy") as Tanner and [Fenella Fielding](/wiki/Fenella_Fielding "Fenella Fielding") as Ann.[[47]](#cite_note-genome-48)

In 1996, to celebrate [BBC Radio 3](/wiki/BBC_Radio_3 "BBC Radio 3")'s 50th Anniversary, [Peter Hall](/wiki/Peter_Hall_(director) "Peter Hall (director)") directed an audio production with [Ralph Fiennes](/wiki/Ralph_Fiennes "Ralph Fiennes") as Jack Tanner, [Judi Dench](/wiki/Judi_Dench "Judi Dench") as Mrs Whitefield, [John Wood](/wiki/John_Wood_(English_actor) "John Wood (English actor)") as Mendoza, [Juliet Stevenson](/wiki/Juliet_Stevenson "Juliet Stevenson") as Ann Whitefield, [Nicholas Le Prevost](/wiki/Nicholas_Le_Prevost "Nicholas Le Prevost") as Octavius Robinson and [Jack Davenport](/wiki/Jack_Davenport "Jack Davenport") as Hector Malone.[[47]](#cite_note-genome-48)

### Television

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Man_and_Superman&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Television")]

A 1958 adaptation for television by [Associated Television](/wiki/Associated_Television "Associated Television") featured [Basil Henson](/wiki/Basil_Henson "Basil Henson") as Tanner and Ann Walford as Ann.[[48]](#cite_note-49) [BBC Television](/wiki/BBC_Television "BBC Television") broadcast the play in 1968 with [Eric Porter](/wiki/Eric_Porter "Eric Porter") and [Maggie Smith](/wiki/Maggie_Smith "Maggie Smith") as the protagonists.[[49]](#cite_note-50) In 1983 [Harlech Television](/wiki/Harlech_Television "Harlech Television") filmed and broadcast the Haymarket production with O'Toole in the lead.[[50]](#cite_note-51)
